relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Religious leaders arrive from Jerusalem.
They see common hands and make purity the issue.
Tradition governs how hands are washed.
Marketplace contact triggers more washings and received practices.
The question becomes why disciples do not walk by elder tradition.
Jesus answers with Isaiah: lips near, heart far.
Human commands can masquerade as worship.
The Divine command is left while human tradition is held.
Tradition can become a way to set command aside.
Moses’ command honors father and mother.
Corban language can become a loophole against love.
A tradition meant for devotion blocks care of parents.
The word is voided by handed-down tradition when mercy is lost.
Jesus calls everyone to hear and understand.
The true purity question is not outside-in but inside-out.
A later manuscript echoes the call for ears to hear.
Privately, disciples ask about the parable.
Jesus presses them toward understanding.
Food enters the body, not the heart.
What comes out reveals what is within.
Harmful patterns rise from the heart before they become action.
Desire, greed, deceit, pride, and harmful sight belong to the inner field.
These inside-out movements make a person common/unclean.
Jesus enters the Tyre region and cannot remain hidden.
A mother comes immediately for her daughter.
The Syrophoenician woman asks for release.
The boundary is spoken in bread-and-table language.
She finds mercy even in the crumbs under the table.
Her word opens the way for her daughter’s release.
She returns home and finds the child free.
Jesus moves through Gentile borderlands toward Galilee.
A deaf man with bound speech is brought to him.
Jesus takes him aside privately and ministers through touch.
Looking to heaven, he sighs and says, Be opened.
Hearing opens, the tongue is released, and speech becomes clear.
Silence cannot contain the proclamation.
The people marvel: he does all things beautifully.
